chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Exploring Substances: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral

flashcards.q: What general taste do acidic substances possess?
What general taste do basic substances possess?
How do basic substances feel to the touch?
What do we call substances that are neither acidic nor basic?
What is an indicator?
flashcards.a: A substance used to test whether a sample is acidic or basic by showing a colour change.

flashcards.q: What is the most commonly used natural acid-base indicator?
From which organism is litmus extracted?
In what two forms is litmus commonly available?
flashcards.a: As a solution and as paper strips (red and blue litmus paper).

flashcards.q: What colour does blue litmus paper turn when an acidic solution is added to it?
What colour does red litmus paper turn when a basic solution is added to it?
What effect do neutral substances have on red and blue litmus paper?
flashcards.a: No effect; the colours remain unchanged.

flashcards.q: What organisms associate symbiotically to form lichens?
What is another name for basic lime water?
Which acid is naturally found in lemons?
Which acids are naturally found in amla (Indian gooseberry)?
flashcards.a: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) and citric acid.

flashcards.q: Which acid is naturally found in tamarind?
Is bitter gourd (karela) basic in nature?
flashcards.a: No, even though it tastes bitter, it is not basic.

flashcards.q: What colour does red rose extract turn in an acidic medium?
What colour does red rose extract turn in a basic medium?
Name three natural indicators apart from litmus and red rose.
flashcards.a: Turmeric, red hibiscus (gudhal), and purple cabbage.

flashcards.q: What colour do hydrangea flowers bloom in acidic soil?
What colour do hydrangea flowers bloom in basic soil?
What colour is turmeric paper in its natural state?
What colour does turmeric paper turn when exposed to a basic substance?
What colour does turmeric paper turn when exposed to an acidic or neutral substance?
Why does a turmeric curry stain on a white shirt turn red when washed with soap?
flashcards.a: Because soap is basic in nature and turmeric acts as an indicator, turning red in basic mediums.

flashcards.q: What is an olfactory indicator?
flashcards.a: A substance whose characteristic odour changes depending on whether it is in an acidic or a basic medium.

flashcards.q: Give one example of an olfactory indicator.
What happens to the smell of an onion in an acidic medium?
What happens to the smell of an onion in a basic medium?
flashcards.a: The characteristic smell is suppressed or changes.

flashcards.q: Who is known as the 'Father of Modern Indian Chemistry'?
What happens when an acidic solution is mixed with a basic solution in suitable proportions?
flashcards.a: They cancel each other's effects in a neutralisation reaction.

flashcards.q: What are the three products of a neutralisation reaction?
During a neutralisation reaction, why does the test tube become warm?
flashcards.a: Because heat is always evolved (released) during the neutralisation process.

flashcards.q: What acid does an ant inject into the skin when it bites?
How can the pain of an ant bite be treated chemically?
flashcards.a: By rubbing moist baking soda, which is a base that neutralises the formic acid.

flashcards.q: What causes agricultural soil to become excessively acidic?
How is excessively acidic soil treated by farmers?
flashcards.a: By adding basic substances, such as lime (calcium oxide/hydroxide), to neutralise the acid.

flashcards.q: How is excessively basic soil treated in agriculture?
flashcards.a: By adding organic matter (manure or compost) which releases acids as it decomposes to neutralise the base.

flashcards.q: Why must an industrial factory's acidic wastewater be treated before discharging it into a river?
flashcards.a: Because highly acidic water is toxic and can kill fish and other aquatic organisms.

flashcards.q: How is acidic factory waste managed?
flashcards.a: Basic substances are added to neutralise the acid before the waste is safely released.

flashcards.q: True or False: Synthetic indicators are naturally found in plants.
flashcards.a: False. Synthetic indicators are made artificially in laboratories.

flashcards.q: What type of salt is produced by a neutralisation reaction?
flashcards.a: The formed salt can be acidic, basic, or neutral in nature, depending on the acid and base used.
